exemplar.title:
How does the quality of life change, as the distance from the
central business district in Warsaw, Poland increases?

How does the quality of life change, as the distance from the
central business district in Warsaw, Poland increases?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is clearly geographical, focused on spatial variation of quality of life, and linked to urban stress theory with testable hypotheses.
Precise transect across Warsaw with five named districts and clearly feasible primary data collection periods.
Geographic context is wellâjustified through onâsite surveys and ties to Urban Environments and Changing Population units.
weaknesses: Maps lack essential conventions (scale bar, north arrow) and one transect map is pixelated with no legend explaining district boundaries.
Geographical background section lacks deeper engagement with urbanâgeography theoretical models (e.g., Burgess, suburbanisation).
Operational definition of âquality of lifeâ is broad and would benefit from more precise measurable indicators.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The question and context strands fully meet topâband descriptors, demonstrating clear theory linkage and precise location identification. Mapping falls short of topâband conventions, but overall the criterion performance is strongest in question formulation and context, meriting the maximum 3 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Multiple methods (Likert questionnaire, noise app, AQI data, transport counts) are described and explicitly linked to each hypothesis.
Data collection techniques were correctly implemented in the field with primary and secondary sources.
Methods section demonstrates adequate understanding of relevance to the research question.
weaknesses: Sample size is small (n=50) with minimal justification of representativeness or randomization strategy.
Reliance on an unvalidated decibelâmeter app introduces potential measurement error without calibration details.
Explanation of fieldwork timing and site selection lacks systematic sampling or detailed justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are described with clear relevance to the research question and correctly applied, placing the work solidly in the mid band (2). However, the lack of sampling justification, small data set, and absence of methodological calibration prevent a band-3 award.

strengths: All data collected are directly relevant to the research question, enabling coherent analysis.
Appropriate presentation techniques (tables, bar charts, maps, photos) aid comprehension of findings.
Most conventions (figure numbering, captions) are correctly used, supporting clear data interpretation.
weaknesses: Sample sufficiency is limited by small n and reliance on perceptions, constraining depth of analysis.
Several figures are low resolution or mislabeled and axis units are sometimes missing.
Formatting inconsistencies (decimal separators, reference styles) and uncited in-text theory reduce professional polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission demonstrates more than minimal partial data sufficiency and uses generally appropriate presentation methods with only occasional convention errors. While not âmost effectiveâ in all respects, the performance reaches the upper half of the mid band, warranting 4 out of 6.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Descriptive statistics (means) are correctly calculated and trends are clearly presented in bar charts.
Key anomalies (e.g., Bemowo transport, Ochota green space) are identified and linked back to hypotheses and urban stress theory.
Discussion revisits and evaluates nearly all hypotheses, demonstrating coherent linking of patterns to theoretical concepts and methods.
weaknesses: No inferential statistical tests (e.g., Spearmanâs rank) or confidence levels are provided to quantify significance.
Analysis lacks depth in explaining sampling bias and does not triangulate anomalies with additional demographic or temporal data.
Some gaps remain in linking secondary AQI data to broader temporal patterns.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While inferential statistics are absent, the student goes beyond basic descriptive analysis by thoroughly identifying trends and anomalies and integrating them with theory. This level of conceptual linking places the work at the lower end of the 5â6 band, justifying 5 out of 8.

strengths: Conclusion clearly summarises findings for each hypothesis and is directly supported by the analysis.
Logical linkage between discussion results and final conclusions is evident.
The conclusion is concise and focused on the research question outcomes.
weaknesses: Conclusion restates qualitative findings without quantitative specificity or reference to anomaly significance.
Does not integrate numerical results or confidence considerations to strengthen support.
Misses an opportunity to highlight the broader geographic implications of the findings.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion fully addresses and is supported by the analysis, fulfilling the topâband descriptor for Criterion E. However, it lacks quantitative depth, so it earns the maximum 2 marks.

strengths: Evaluation outlines relevant strengths (resident interviews, reputable secondary sources) and weaknesses (small sample, non-cooperative respondents).
Proposes feasible improvements such as larger sample sizes and inclusion of socio-economic variables.
Acknowledges limitations of AQI data and suggests methods to improve data robustness.
weaknesses: Discussion of improvements lacks detailed explanation of how changes would impact data quality and analysis.
Strengths and weaknesses are listed but not explored in depth or linked to specific methodological outcomes.
Does not quantify the expected effect of proposed improvements (e.g., error reduction, increased validity).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation appropriately outlines relevant strengths, weaknesses, and improvement suggestions, aligning with band-2 descriptors. The lack of comprehensive explanation of impact or detailed evaluation prevents a band-3 award.

strandSelections.reasoning: Maps of Poland, Warsaw and the transect are included and labelled, but several lack essential conventions (scale bars, north arrow, clear legend). The transect map is helpful yet slightly pixelated and boundaries are not fully explained. Hence mapping is appropriate but does not fully achieve the clarity/convention standard of the top band.

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, focuses on spatial variation of quality-of-life and is explicitly related to Urban Environments and Changing Population units. The question lends itself to hypothesis testing (three hypotheses are stated) and is worded precisely enough to direct an investigation. The student also defines the key concept of âquality of lifeâ and links it to recognised urban-geography theory (urban stress). This meets the top-band expectation.

Tables, bar charts, photos and maps are used appropriately and aid comprehension. They are, however, basic (no correlation plots, GIS or proportional symbols) and some figures are low-resolution. Thus presentation is suitable but not the âmost effectiveâ, warranting a 3 within the 3-4 band.

Techniques were correctly applied (primary interviews, decibel app, desktop counts), but the dataset is small (n=50) and lacks detail on randomisation or calibration, limiting reliability. Implementation is adequate rather than âhigh-qualityâ, placing it in the middle band.

The student employs descriptive statistics (means) and simple bar charts yet no inferential tests (e.g., Spearmanâs rank). Hence techniques are descriptive and appropriate, but the absence of statistical rigour limits the work to the 1-2 band; the higher mark (2) reflects correct calculations where attempted.

Trends and some anomalies (Bemowo transport, Ochota green space) are identified and linked back to the hypotheses and concepts such as urban stress. The explanation is coherent but not exhaustive nor underpinned by quantitative significance tests, fitting the 3-4 band at its lower end.

A specific transect across Warsaw (ÅrÃ³dmieÅcie â Ursus) is identified, five study districts are named and justified, and the feasibility for primary data collection is demonstrated by on-site surveys during two clearly dated periods. This precision and feasibility satisfy the descriptors for the highest band.

The evaluation acknowledges strengths (resident interviews, reputable secondary sources) and weaknesses (small sample, non-cooperative respondents) but explanations are brief. This aligns with an âappropriate outlineâ rather than comprehensive treatment.

All data collected relate directly to the research question, yet the sample size and reliance on perceptions mean sufficiency is only partial. The evidence is workable for analysis but not extensive, aligning with the 3-4 band; a mid-point score of 3 is appropriate.

A clear conclusion summarises findings against each hypothesis and is broadly supported by the preceding discussion. Although justification is qualitative and not statistically validated, the linkage is evident, matching the full-support descriptor (band 2).

Most hypotheses are revisited and discussed, but gaps remain: no analysis of sampling bias, limited discussion of AQI anomaly, and no triangulation. Consequently, the analysis is competent with some omissions, meriting 3 within the 3-4 band.

Methods (Likert-scale questionnaire, noise-app readings, secondary AQI data, public-transport counts) are described and explicitly linked to each hypothesis. However, sampling strategy (only 10 respondents per district) and justification of timing/ sites are brief, so the explanation is clear but not fully comprehensive.
Most figures are numbered and captioned, but referencing is inconsistent (e.g., âfigure 5â mis-labelled; missing sources for Google maps). Such occasional errors match the middle band descriptor.

Suggested improvements (larger sample, longer interviews, include socio-economic variables) are relevant and feasible, yet the impact on results is not fully analysed. This corresponds to the middle band: relevant suggestions outlined without detailed effect assessment.

annotations.comment: The link to the syllabus mentions units but lacks precise theoretical rationale for choosing urban stress concepts, limiting depth of geographic context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Definition of âquality of lifeâ is broad; consider operationalizing it with measurable indicators (e.g., specific noise thresholds, greenâspace area per capita) for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question is clearly geographical and allows hypothesis formation by linking âquality of lifeâ to urban stress theory, providing a strong focus for the investigation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The map of Warsaw lacks a scale bar and north arrow, which are essential conventions for clear spatial orientation and accurate distance interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The transect map (Fig. 2) is pixelated and missing a clear legend explaining district boundaries; improving resolution and labelling would enhance its utility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The Likertâscale survey categories are listed but lack guidance on how midâpoints are interpreted; clarify how numerical ratings (1â5) translate into qualityâofâlife scores.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Reliance on an unvalidated decibelâmeter app may introduce measurement error; consider calibrating against professional equipment or documenting app accuracy.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The screenshot of the decibel app is uncaptioned and missing source details; include a figure caption explaining date, location and app version for proper documentation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The narrative around Hypothesis 1 is descriptive but lacks any statistical analysis (e.g., mean comparisons, Spearmanâs rank) to quantify whether differences are significant.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Numbering in the hypotheses list is inconsistent (1., 2., 3.) and formatting shifts midâitem; standardize numbering and ensure each hypothesis is clearly separated.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The geographical background describes recent metro expansions but lacks engagement with urban geography theory (e.g., Burgess model, suburbanisation) to contextualize growth patterns.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Sampling only 10 respondents per district yields a small dataset (n=50), limiting statistical reliability; justify why this sample is representative or expand sample size.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Describing fieldwork as âmobileâ lacks detail on how locations were selected in each district; specify randomization or systematic sampling to strengthen validity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Fig. 6 is referred to but the table caption on page 8 is ambiguous, lacking clarity on variable definitions; ensure all figures are explicitly numbered and described.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Decimal separators alternate between commas and periods in the survey results table, which can confuse interpretation; standardize numeric formatting throughout.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Conclusion restates findings but lacks quantitative specificity and does not address the significance of anomalies; integrate key numerical results to strengthen support.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: Evaluation outlines general improvements but lacks depth on how suggested changes (longer interviews, socioâeconomic variables) would impact data quality and analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Interpretation acknowledges some anomalies but remains at surface level; deepen discussion by linking specific variables to urban stress theory with quantitative evidence.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Bar charts are low resolution and missing axis labels (units, scales); improve figure quality and ensure axes are clearly annotated for reader comprehension.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Urban stress concept is mentioned but lacks inâtext citation; reference academic sources when invoking theory to strengthen theoretical underpinnings.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Public transport table uses a single data snapshot without triangulation or trend analysis; consider comparing multiple time periods or sources for reliability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The explanation for the BemowoâOchota anomaly is largely anecdotal; bolster with demographic or travelâpattern data to substantiate the reasoning.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Green space assessment relies on photos and selfâreported survey but lacks objective metrics (e.g., % green cover); integrate GIS or official landâuse data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Photographs of green spaces lack scale bars, location labels or orientation arrows, reducing their usefulness in spatial analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Air quality index table uses a single online source and does not account for temporal variability; consider longitudinal data or multiple monitoring stations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Limitations of the AQI data are acknowledged but not mitigated; propose methods to control for weather or diurnal fluctuations to improve data robustness.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Reference list contains inconsistent formats and Wikipedia entries; apply a consistent citation style and prioritize peerâreviewed or authoritative sources.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
